$note: Proposed additions from the indefinite batch. Checked against data/glossary.json v8 (terms AND aliases) before proposing: none of the four exists under any spelling. Architecture owns glossary.json; these are proposals, not edits (stay-in-role).

indefinite.short: A word that points at some, any, every or no one, without identifying which: qualche, alcuni, ogni, qualcuno, nessuno, chiunque.
indefinite.long: Indefinites are the words that quantify without naming. Some of them stand alone as pronouns (qualcuno ha telefonato), some lean on a noun as adjectives (ogni giorno), and a few do both (nessuno). What ties them together is that they leave the identity open: qualcuno is somebody, but you are not saying who. Their machinery varies sharply -- qualche and ogni force a singular noun, alcuni takes a plural, tutto keeps the article -- so they are learnt one at a time rather than as a set.

distributive.short: Picks out the members of a group ONE BY ONE, and so takes a singular verb: ognuno di noi ha un compito.
distributive.long: A distributive indefinite runs through a group individually rather than gathering it up. Ognuno and ciascuno are the Italian pair, and the singular verb is the visible consequence: ognuno di noi HA un compito, never hanno, because the subject is ognuno and not noi. The contrast is with the collective tutti, which takes the group whole and so takes a plural verb: tutti hanno un compito. Same fact about the world, opposite agreement.

free choice.short: Any at all, whichever you pick: qualunque, qualsiasi, chiunque, dovunque. In a subordinate clause they pull the subjunctive.
free choice.long: A free-choice word says the choice is open and does not matter: qualsiasi ora means any hour you like, not every hour. Italian has an adjectival pair (qualunque, qualsiasi, which need a noun), a pronoun (chiunque, which never takes one), and a place word (dovunque). Open a subordinate clause with any of them and the subjunctive follows: chiunque sia, qualunque cosa faccia. Put qualunque AFTER its noun and the meaning turns dismissive instead: un vino qualunque is a wine like any other.

apocope.short: Dropping a word's final vowel or syllable before another word: uno -> un amico, nessuno -> nessun amico, ciascuno -> ciascun candidato.
apocope.long: Apocope is the clipping Italian does to keep words running smoothly into each other. The indefinite article is the pattern everything else copies: un amico, un libro, but uno zaino and uno studente, because z and s + consonant resist the clip. Nessuno and ciascuno follow it exactly, which is why nessun amico and nessuno studente look inconsistent until you notice they are simply tracking the article. Before a feminine vowel the clip becomes an elision and takes an apostrophe: un'idea, nessun'idea.

rationale.indefinite: The topic's own name, used across all 61 explanations. Never defined anywhere in the estate.
rationale.distributive: Named in the ognuno/ciascuno leaf's label-adjacent description and load-bearing on the topic's sixth hot spot (the singular verb). The tutti-vs-ognuno agreement contrast is unteachable without the term.
rationale.free choice: The name of one of my three aggregates. Used in the free_choice explanations and in the mood-seam thread; currently opaque to a learner.
rationale.apocope: The estate has 'clipped word' (for words like cinema/foto) and 'elision', but neither covers the uno/nessun/ciascun clipping, which is the second hot spot of this topic and reappears in the article, possessive and adjective trees (buon, gran, san). Checked: 'apocope' is absent as term AND alias. Adjacent to the existing 'elision' entry; the two should probably cross-reference.
